---
roa-num: 646.GN-FY-XXX
submitted-to:
  person: First Last, Title
  association: Group or Division Name
cover-title:
  line1: Title Line 1
  # Including `line2` is optional; set to `false` if not needed
  line2: false
purpose: Brief statement specifying purpose of the ROA
lab-dates:
  from:
    day: 1
    month: 1
    year: 9999
  to:
    day: 1
    month: 1
    year: 9999
methods: Sample preparation and/or instrumental methods used
controls-used: Control samples used
quality-docs: Applicable quality manuals and technical procedures
# Optional metadata begin -----------------------------------------------------
# The following should be specified only if applicable; set to `false` if not needed
srms: false
constituents: false
assigned-value:
  value: false
  uncertainty: false
  # `units` should be specified by their common name (e.g., ppm, ppb, percent, or %)
  units: false
exp-date:
  day: false
  month: false
  year: false
collaborators: false
agreements: false
approvals: false
copies-to: false
# Optional metadata end -------------------------------------------------------
# Email address is assumed to be first.last@nist.gov. If email is different from
# this convention, an `email` field can be provided below `title`.
prepared-by:
  - name: Brandon LeBlanc
    title: Physical Science Technician
reviewed-by:
  - name: Reviewer Name
    title: Reviewer Title
---

# Introduction

This introduction is designed to give a brief overview of common markdown
formatting tasks with Quarto. It is not designed to be exhaustive; the [Quarto
guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide) will be able to provide more examples and
information.

*italic text*, **bold text**, ***bold italic text***, super^script^, sub~script~

Headings are shown throughout the document on the lines that begin with \#. One
\# gives a level 1 heading, two \#s give a level 2 heading, etc.

Quarto reserves certain symbols to specify markdown directives throughout the
document, such as \* and \#. To insert these in normal text, they must be
escaped (prepend a backslash).

- Bulleted lists
- must have a blank
- line separating them
  - from the surrounding text
    - or they won't
- render properly

For unordered lists, you can either use the symbols that the Quarto documents
show, or you can just use a regular hyphen. Both will work, as long as the
correct spacing is maintained.

Equations use LaTex syntax. You can have equations as inline math: $E = mc^{2}$.

They can also be specified in their own block (display math):

$$
i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial{t}} \Psi \left( x, t \right)
  = \left[ -\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}
  \frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial{x^{2}}} + V
  \right] \Psi \left( x, t \right)
$$ {#eq-tdse}

@eq-tdse shows the Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation, rendered in its own
block.

Here's a second equation example:

$$
a^2 + b^2 = c^2
$$ {#eq-pythag}

@eq-pythag shows the Pythagorean Theorem.

Tables can be created in a variety of ways.

  | header 1    | header 2    | header 3    |
  | :---------: | :---------: | :---------: |
  | row 1 col 1 | row 1 col 2 | row 1 col 3 |
  | row 2 col 1 | row 2 col 2 | row 2 col 3 |

  : Example of a pipe table. {#tbl-pipe-example tbl-align="center"}

@tbl-pipe-example gives an example of a [pipe
table](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/tables.html#markdown-tables), which is
the most common way of specifying a markdown table. The number of hyphens used
indicate the relative widths of the columns. The pipes do not have to be aligned
with one another or spaced consistently. The cell contents of pipe tables can
only be single-line elements, and keeping track of the formatting gets pretty
cumbersome.

::: {#tbl-list-example .list-table aligns="c"}
Example of a list table.

- - header 1
  - header 2
  - header 3

- - row 1 col 1
  - row 1 col 2
  - row 1 col 3

- - row 2 col 1
  - row 2 col 2
  - row 2 col 3
:::

@tbl-list-example gives an example of a [list
table](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/tables.html#list-tables). This format
is much more readable and user-friendly for entering the data. Each top-level
item indicates a new row, and each nested item is a cell in that row. The cell
contents of list tables can be any content, including block elements (lists,
code cells, etc.) and multiline paragraphs.

There are also [grid
tables](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/tables.html#grid-tables), which are
similar to pipe tables but allow for arbitrary elements in the cells.
Additionally, tables can be rendered programmatically using R or Python, which
can be useful for reading in data from another file. This option requires using
packages to output the data into a markdown format, and more details can be
found [here](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/tables.html#computations).

Inserting figures is more straightforward, but there are a lot of layout
customization options.

![Defining the International System of Units
(SI).](https://www.nist.gov/sites/default/files/images/2018/09/11/si_illustration_constants_colour_full.png){#fig-si fig-align="center" width=50%}

@fig-si gives an example of inserting a figure with a caption. There are lots of
ways to customize the layout of figures into multiple sub-figures, rows,
columns, and panels. Like tables, figures can also be generated programmatically
from R or Python, which is useful for including complex data visualizations in a
report. Again, this requires the use of packages to generate the figures. More
information on layouts and computed figures can be found
[here](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/figures.html).

Throughout this introduction,
[cross-references](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/cross-references.html) were
used on equations, tables, and figures by assigning them a label beginning with
\# (e.g., \#eq-tdse). These labels can be used in the body to reference the
specific item by replacing the \# with @. Equation labels must be prefixed with
'eq', table labels with 'tbl', and figure labels with 'fig'. Quarto will
automatically keep track of the order they appear in the text and keep the
references current should that order change.

**TO DO:**

- More examples for utilizing
  [references](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html)
  - See the link to get started, and note that there is already a 'refs.bib'
    file in this template that can be updated with the relevant sources.
- Add examples of computations for tables and figures.
  - [R info](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html)
  - [Python info](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/python.html)
- Add examples for
  [shortcodes](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/shortcodes.html), specifically
  for \`include\`, \`pagebreak\`, and possibly \`contents\`.

# Traceability Statement

{{< lipsum 1 >}}

# Experimental

## Safety

Hazard Reviews.

## Materials

{{< lipsum 1 >}}

## Sample Preparation

{{< lipsum 1 >}}

## Equipment and Instrumentation

- Instrument/Equipment 1
- Instrument/Equipment 2
- Instrument/Equipment 3

## Instrumental Methods

{{< lipsum 2 >}}

## Quantitative Methods

### Statistical Analysis

{{< lipsum 1 >}}

### Homogeneity Analysis

{{< lipsum 2 >}}

### Stability

{{< lipsum 1 >}}

# Results and Discussion

## Value Assignment of Lot Standards

{{< lipsum 2 >}}

### Analysis of Previous Lot Standards

{{< lipsum 3 >}}

## Value Assignment of Samples

{{< lipsum 2 >}}

## Uncertainty Assessment

{{< lipsum 2 >}}

# Disposition of Cylinders

{{< lipsum 2 >}}

# Data Storage

- Notebook 1
- File 1
- File 2

# References

::: {.make-refs}
{{< meta references >}} {{< meta references-style >}}
:::
